narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dreams despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPAs, family income levels, and whether they plan to study full-time—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it calculated a 64% probability that you might not pass the bar initially, which is high enough to trigger a rejection as the school aims to admit those with stronger predicted odds of success. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to peers, signaling to the model a potential struggle in advanced coursework that often correlates with bar performance. Your LSAT score of 20, well below the typical 37, was the second strongest influence in the same direction, as lower scores like yours have historically linked to higher bar failure rates in the data. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7—slightly under the average 3.2—actually nudged the prediction toward success, showing some resilience in your college work. Your upper-middle family income also helped a bit, as students from similar backgrounds tend to have better outcomes, along with your full-time study plan and decent Year 1 decile of 2, both of which slightly favored passing. Compared to typical students, your profile stands out with lower early and late rankings plus that LSAT, putting you below the averages where most successful bar passers cluster. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it significantly, strengthening your later academic deciles with extra study habits, and perhaps seeking mentorship programs tailored for underrepresented students to build a standout reapplication. You're capable of turning this around, and reapplying with improvements could change the model's view entirely. Keep pushing; your story matters.
